term_glossary.tooltip: Battery Energy Storage System or Systems.
Hybrid Energy Storage System or Systems.
Authority Having Jurisdiction; the local authority that can approve, reject, or add requirements to an ESS deployment.
Hazard Mitigation Analysis; an engineering review of credible ESS failure modes and the safety systems intended to mitigate them.

slides.title: The Shift In ESS Deployment

source_refs.source: slides.pptx
ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt

slides.subtitle: ESS is moving from a singular facility asset toward distributed storage layers.

bullets.text: Energy storage now appears at facility, row, rack, sidecar, and load-adjacent layers.
Each placement affects fire protection, operations, maintenance access, and approval.
Teams need common terms before evaluating safety and compliance.

figure.alt: Line diagram showing ESS moving from centralized UPS toward distributed storage layers.
figure.caption: Summit framing: ESS adoption is spreading across more layers of the data center power architecture.

slides.title: Course Boundary

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt

slides.subtitle: The course teaches how to reason through requirements, not how to select a vendor.

bullets.text: Behind-the-meter data center ESS deployment, hazards, standards, and operating considerations.
The white paper stays neutral on chemistry and vendor selection.
Grid-tied interconnection can add utility, DSO, and TSO requirements beyond this course.
What must be planned before safety or approval constraints force late rework?

slides.title: Why AI Changes The Stakes

source_refs.source: slides.pptx
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Fast power changes increase the value of local buffering and the cost of poor planning.

bullets.text: Storage functions range from nanosecond smoothing to hours of backup.
AI clusters can make load behavior more dynamic and local power consequences larger.
Resilience gains must be balanced with electrical, thermal, gas, and operational hazards.

figure.alt: Storage response time continuum from capacitors to generators.
figure.caption: Use case timing helps determine which storage layer is relevant.

slides.title: Stakeholders And Decision Points

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: ESS is a facility decision with design, operations, risk, approval, and response implications.

cards.title: Design and Build
cards.body: Electrical architecture, mechanical layout, fire protection, space planning, and integration constraints.

cards.title: Operations
cards.body: Monitoring, maintenance, procedures, training, PPE, availability, and lifecycle records.

cards.title: Business and Risk
cards.body: Resilience, cost, sustainability, liability, insurance, and total cost of ownership.

cards.title: Authorities and Insurers
cards.body: Permitting, local acceptance, amendments, loss-prevention rules, and approval conditions.

cards.title: Response and Community
cards.body: First responder planning plus OCP feedback that improves future guidance.

slides.title: What Learners Should Watch For

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Use these prompts throughout the course to keep the requirements practical.

bullets.text: Facility, row, rack, sidecar, indoor room, outdoor yard, white space, or grey space.
Battery chemistry, supercapacitor, capacitor, or hybrid system.
Electrical, thermal, gas, explosion, chemical, and fire propagation paths.
Codes, product standards, installation standards, regional rules, and insurer guidance.
AHJ, certifiers, insurer, facility operations, and emergency response stakeholders.

slides.title: Module 1 Summary

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

items: ESS is becoming part of the core data center power architecture.
The white paper is a practical deployment guide, not a vendor or chemistry recommendation.
Distributed storage distributes responsibility across more stakeholders.
Start with use case and location before mapping hazards and standards.
Early safety and approval planning prevents late design rework.

slides.title: Check The Planning Mindset
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: When applying ESS requirements, which planning move should come first?

choices.text: Define the use case and location before mapping hazards, standards, and approvals.
Select a vendor and chemistry first, then adapt the facility plan around that product.
Wait until the authority having jurisdiction asks for a specific document.
Treat ESS as an IT accessory with no separate facility or response planning.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Use case and location shape the hazard review, standards path, stakeholders, and approval work.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Start with use case and location. Vendor or chemistry choices do not replace early hazard, standards, and approval planning.

questions.prompt: Which stakeholder groups should be part of early ESS planning?

choices.text: Design, build, and facility operations teams.
Authorities, insurers, and emergency response stakeholders.
Business and risk owners responsible for resilience and liability.
Only the procurement team after final equipment selection.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Distributed ESS affects design, operations, risk, approvals, and response.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Early ESS planning is cross-functional. It should include design, operations, risk, authorities, insurers, and response stakeholders.

slides.title: Up Next
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 1: Why ESS Requirements Matter In AI Data Centers.

slides.title: Deployment Models, Architectures, And Technologies
slides.subtitle: How ESS placement, coupling, control, and storage technology shape the compliance path.

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Deployment Choices You Will Classify

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to...
objectives: Distinguish row/rack, facility-level, microgrid, and edge ESS deployment models.
Classify ESS architecture by coupling, distribution, control, orientation, hybridization, and grid interaction.
Compare technology families without turning the white paper into a product recommendation.
Explain why use case, location, and technology shape safety and compliance review.

slides.title: Row, Rack, And Sidecar ESS

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Storage closer to the IT load changes both resilience and hazard analysis.

bullets.text: Shared energy storage can support several racks or a rack-level domain.
Storage adjacent to rack infrastructure can support distributed UPS behavior.
Supercapacitors may smooth short GPU or TPU power spikes.
Proximity to IT equipment raises access, isolation, detection, ventilation, and response questions.

figure.alt: Summit visual showing grid, facility-level ESS, row-level ESS, rack-level ESS, and IT equipment.
figure.caption: Summit slide 5: ESS can appear across facility, row, rack, and equipment layers.

slides.title: Facility-Level ESS

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Large systems often interface with main low-voltage or medium-voltage switchgear.

bullets.text: Facility-level ESS can provide backup power when grid supply is lost.
Systems greater than 100 kW may locate in mechanical rooms or containerized installations.
Outdoor systems may require land, foundations, clearances, roads, and responder access.
Centralized storage can simplify some domains but increase impact if a large load domain is affected.

figure.alt: White paper Figure 2 showing illustrative data center power systems with ESS.
figure.caption: White paper Figure 2: current and emerging data center ESS deployment models.

slides.title: Microgrid And Edge Models

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Grid-interactive behavior changes the approval path.

bullets.text: Microgrid sites may combine local generation with ESS and facility loads.
Controls may support operation independent of grid supply.
Utility, DSO, or TSO requirements can apply when the system interacts with the grid.

figure.alt: Microgrid diagram with grid, local generation, ESS, and data center load.
figure.caption: Classify grid interaction early; backup-only and grid-interactive systems can follow different approval paths.
figure.svg:

slides.title: Charging Energy Is A Site Load

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Recharge behavior is part of the facility load model.

bullets.text: Unmanaged ESS charging adds to IT and other facility power usage.
Recharge can exceed equipment or grid connection limits if unplanned.
Many distributed nodes recharging together can create new site peaks.
Define when recharge happens, what limits it, and how it behaves after an event.

figure.alt: Stacked bar chart showing ESS recharge added to IT and facility load.
figure.caption: ESS charging is not invisible; it consumes site capacity.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 720 330" role="img" aria-label="ESS recharge load adds to site load" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:330px;">

  <text x="34" y="42" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="22" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Charging Energy Is Additive</text>
  <line x1="86" y1="270" x2="650" y2="270" stroke="#5F6062" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="86" y1="270" x2="86" y2="76" stroke="#5F6062" stroke-width="2"/>
  <g><rect x="138" y="168" width="90" height="102" fill="#343895"/><rect x="138" y="118" width="90" height="50" fill="#8DC63F"/><rect x="138" y="96" width="90" height="22" fill="#A6D86B"/></g>
  <g><rect x="314" y="142" width="90" height="128" fill="#343895"/><rect x="314" y="96" width="90" height="46" fill="#8DC63F"/><rect x="314" y="68" width="90" height="28" fill="#A6D86B"/></g>
  <g><rect x="490" y="176" width="90" height="94" fill="#343895"/><rect x="490" y="128" width="90" height="48" fill="#8DC63F"/><rect x="490" y="84" width="90" height="44" fill="#A6D86B"/></g>
  <g font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="14" fill="#5F6062" text-anchor="middle"><text x="183" y="298">normal</text><text x="359" y="298">AI peak</text><text x="535" y="298">recharge window</text></g>
  <g font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="13" fill="#343895"><rect x="612" y="94" width="18" height="18" fill="#343895"/><text x="638" y="109">IT load</text><rect x="612" y="126" width="18" height="18" fill="#8DC63F"/><text x="638" y="141">facility load</text><rect x="612" y="158" width="18" height="18" fill="#A6D86B"/><text x="638" y="173">ESS recharge</text></g>

slides.title: Architecture And Control Topologies

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Use consistent labels before starting standards and hazard analysis.
rows: Determines interface, conversion, protection, and applicable standards.
Defines fault domains, exposure, and consequence.
Affects protection, energy management, and site-level coordination.
Clarifies whether runtime or short-duration response is primary.
Changes hazard profile and control complexity.
Can trigger utility and regional interconnection rules.

slides.title: Storage Technologies And Tradeoffs

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: The paper compares roles and tradeoffs, not winners and losers.

cards.title: Capacitors
cards.body: Point-of-load decoupling, hold-up energy, ripple handling, and very fast response.

cards.title: Supercapacitors
cards.body: Bridge power, pulse buffering, power quality, high cycle life, and short-duration response.

cards.title: Lithium-ion variants (Li-ion)
cards.body: Includes LFP / LiFePO4 and NMC / LiNiMnCoO2 examples, with thermal runaway and approval considerations.

cards.title: Nickel-zinc and lead-acid
cards.body: Includes aqueous nickel batteries (ANB), nickel-zinc (NiZn), VRLA, and TPPL options with different maintenance and safety profiles.

cards.title: Sodium-ion variants
cards.body: SIB / Na-ion technologies are emerging electrochemical options with technology-specific testing and deployment considerations.

cards.title: Mechanical / gravity
cards.body: Possible storage categories but less common in typical data center operations due to fit and runtime concerns.

slides.title: Deployment Summary

source_refs.source: slides.pptx

items: Deployment location changes hazard analysis and approval needs.
Architecture labels make ESS review specific enough to act on.
Charging energy is a facility load and must be managed.
Technology comparisons should remain neutral and use-case specific.
The right question is fit for this use case, location, risk profile, and approval path.

slides.title: Check Deployment Tradeoffs
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Why should ESS charging behavior be included in the facility load model?

choices.text: Unmanaged recharge can add to site load, exceed limits, or create new peaks.
Charging energy is never counted as part of data center power usage.
It applies only to outdoor containerized systems.
It removes the need to review electrical architecture.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Recharge behavior can become a site load event, especially across many distributed nodes.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Review the charging-energy slide. Recharge can add to facility demand and must be limited, scheduled, and modeled.

questions.prompt: Which labels help classify an ESS architecture before hazard and standards review?

choices.text: Electrical coupling: DC-coupled or AC-coupled.
System distribution: distributed or centralized.
Grid interaction: backup-only or grid-interactive.
The color of the enclosure finish.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Architecture labels make the review specific enough to connect to hazards, controls, and approval paths.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Use technical architecture labels: coupling, distribution, control, orientation, hybridization, and grid interaction.

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Safety Factors You Will Evaluate

source_refs.source: slides.pptx

slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to...
objectives: Describe the electrical hazard baseline and the voltage ranges highlighted in the paper.
Recognize thermal, gas, explosion, chemical, and fire propagation hazard categories.
Connect hazards to location, separation, detection, ventilation, suppression, and testing controls.
Explain why trained personnel, PPE, emergency planning, and CSA considerations are part of ESS safety.

slides.title: Electrical Hazard Baseline

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Treat ESS as energized until a qualified process proves otherwise.

bullets.text: NFPA 70E identifies shock hazard at voltages greater than 50 volts.
The paper notes data center ESS moving toward 400-1500 volts with hot-swap capabilities.
Only trained personnel should handle, service, or operate ESS using appropriate PPE and supervision.

figure.alt: Voltage gauge showing greater-than-50-volts threshold and 400-1500 volt ESS range.
figure.caption: Electrical hazard planning starts before modern ESS operating voltages.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 720 320" role="img" aria-label="Electrical hazard threshold and ESS operating range" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:320px;">
  <rect width="720" height="320" fill="#ffffff"/>
  <text x="34" y="42" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="22" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Electrical Hazard Baseline</text>
  <line x1="78" y1="178" x2="648" y2="178" stroke="#5F6062" stroke-width="4" stroke-linecap="round"/>
  <rect x="120" y="146" width="470" height="64" rx="32" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="4"/>
  <line x1="170" y1="122" x2="170" y2="230" stroke="#343895" stroke-width="4"/><text x="170" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">> 50 V</text><text x="170" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="13" fill="#5F6062">shock hazard threshold</text>
  <line x1="350" y1="122" x2="350" y2="230" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="590" y1="122" x2="590" y2="230" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="470" y="106" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">400-1500 V</text><text x="470" y="250" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="13" fill="#5F6062">modern ESS operating range noted by paper</text>
</svg>

slides.title: Thermal, Gas, Explosion, And Chemical Hazards

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt

slides.subtitle: A credible review looks beyond fire alone.

bullets.text: Thermal runaway and fire propagation are central concerns for some chemistries.
Hydrogen, oxygen, toxic gases, or combustible mixtures can drive ventilation and explosion controls.
Electrolyte leakage and toxic byproducts depend on chemistry and failure mode.
Shock, arc flash, and energized maintenance conditions require controls and procedures.

figure.alt: Summit hazard taxonomy for electrical, thermal, gas and explosion, chemical, integration, standards, and installation issues.
figure.caption: Summit slide 6 groups hazards and deployment issues for ESS review.

slides.title: Placing ESS To Reduce Risk

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Location is one of the strongest risk controls.

bullets.text: The paper references FM Global guidance for a minimum 2-hour fire-rated room in some battery installations.
Distance from combustibles, accessible sides, and adjacent racks can depend on evidence and approval.
Solid barriers can reduce fire risk area and help prevent propagation.

figure.alt: Composite of white paper figures showing preferred ESS locations and thermal barriers.
figure.caption: White paper Figures 3 and 4: location, spacing, and barriers affect propagation risk.

slides.title: Detection, Ventilation, And Suppression

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Detection, ventilation, suppression, and explosion control should not be designed in isolation.

bullets.text: Smoke, thermal imaging, radiant-energy, gas, and ventilation-failure alarms may be required.
Natural or mechanical ventilation can remove gases and may need backup power.
Water-based and gas-based systems have different use cases and constraints.
Airflow, gas removal, fire behavior, and suppression effectiveness must be evaluated together.

figure.alt: Flow diagram connecting detection, ventilation, suppression, explosion control, and backup power.
figure.caption: Controls must work together under fault and emergency conditions.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 760 330" role="img" aria-label="Detection ventilation suppression and explosion control flow" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:330px;">
  <rect width="760" height="330" fill="#ffffff"/>
  <text x="34" y="42" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="22" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Controls Must Work As A System</text>
  <defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#343895"/></marker></defs>
  <g font-family="Open Sans, Arial" text-anchor="middle">
    <rect x="42" y="118" width="126" height="72" rx="10" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="105" y="148" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Detection</text><text x="105" y="169" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">smoke / gas / thermal</text>
    <rect x="206" y="118" width="126" height="72" rx="10" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="269" y="148" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Ventilation</text><text x="269" y="169" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">remove gases</text>
    <rect x="370" y="118" width="126" height="72" rx="10" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="433" y="148" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Suppression</text><text x="433" y="169" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">fire control</text>
    <rect x="534" y="118" width="150" height="72" rx="10" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="609" y="148" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Explosion Control</text><text x="609" y="169" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">where applicable</text>
  </g>
  <g stroke="#343895" stroke-width="4" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arrow)"><path d="M168 154 H198"/><path d="M332 154 H362"/><path d="M496 154 H526"/></g>
  <rect x="164" y="238" width="430" height="42" rx="21" fill="#343895"/><text x="379" y="265" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#ffffff">backup power for critical safety subsystems</text>
</svg>

slides.title: Testing And Hazard Mitigation Analysis

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Testing and HMA connect product behavior to site acceptance.

bullets.text: UL 9540A and CSA C800 provide thermal runaway and large-scale fire test evidence paths.
The installation standard addresses location, sizing, ventilation, suppression, explosion control, egress, and lifecycle steps.
Hazard Mitigation Analysis evaluates credible failure modes and survivability of safety systems.
Local acceptance depends on evidence, site design, and authority requirements.

figure.alt: Evidence chain from testing to hazard mitigation analysis to AHJ review and operations.
figure.caption: Evidence packages should connect tests, design assumptions, review, and operating controls.
figure.svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 760 330" role="img" aria-label="Testing and hazard mitigation evidence chain" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:330px;"><rect width="760" height="330" fill="#ffffff"/><text x="34" y="42" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="22" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Evidence Chain For ESS Safety</text><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="10" markerHeight="10" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L9,3 z" fill="#343895"/></marker></defs><rect x="72" y="120" width="122" height="92" rx="12" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="133" y="152" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Test evidence</text><text x="133" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">UL 9540A / CSA C800</text><path d="M198 166 H240" stroke="#343895" stroke-width="4" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="245" y="120" width="122" height="92" rx="12" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="306" y="152" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">HMA</text><text x="306" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">credible failure modes</text><path d="M371 166 H413" stroke="#343895" stroke-width="4" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="418" y="120" width="122" height="92" rx="12" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F" stroke-width="3"/><text x="479" y="152" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="15" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">AHJ review</text><text x="479" y="178" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062">local acceptance</text><path d="M544 166 H586" stroke="#343895" stroke-width="4" fill="none" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/><rect x="591" fill="#343895">Operations</text><text text-anchor="middle" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="12" fill="#5F6062"><tspan x="652" y="174">procedures</tspan><tspan x="652" y="189">and training</tspan></text></svg>

slides.title: Handling, Emergency Response, And CSA

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Safety controls have to remain real after installation.

cards.title: Qualified Work
cards.body: Repairs and servicing require trained personnel, PPE, safety supervision, and documentation.

cards.title: Emergency Response
cards.body: Follow facility and manufacturer procedures; personnel should not improvise around ESS fires.

cards.title: Civil Planning
cards.body: Outdoor systems can require land, foundations, evaluated soils, roads, and responder access.

cards.title: Structural Planning
cards.body: Indoor rack-based lithium-ion BESS may weigh 4,000-6,000 pounds fully loaded.

cards.title: Architectural Separation
cards.body: White and grey space planning should isolate hazards from non-ESS equipment where possible.

cards.title: Penetration Sealing
cards.body: Cable and pipe seals should address structure integrity, gas, water, dust, smoke, and liquid containment.

slides.title: Safety Summary

source_refs.source: slides.pptx

items: ESS safety is a system, not a single device.
Electrical, thermal, gas, explosion, chemical, and fire risks must be evaluated together.
Location, separation, detection, ventilation, suppression, and explosion controls are interdependent.
Testing and hazard mitigation analysis provide evidence for review.
Operations, emergency response, and CSA planning keep the design safe over time.

slides.title: Check Safety Controls
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What is the role of testing and Hazard Mitigation Analysis in ESS safety review?

choices.text: They connect credible failure behavior, safety-system survivability, and site acceptance evidence.
They replace the need for location, ventilation, suppression, and emergency planning.
They are only needed when a site is grid-interactive.
They prove that all ESS technologies have the same hazard profile.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. HMA and testing evidence help reviewers understand credible failures and whether controls survive them.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Testing and HMA do not replace controls. They provide evidence that product behavior, site design, and safety systems fit the risk.

questions.prompt: Which controls should be evaluated together rather than in isolation?

choices.text: Detection and alarm strategy.
Ventilation, gas removal, and backup power for safety subsystems.
Suppression, explosion control, location, and separation.
A generic statement that the system is safe without site evidence.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. ESS safety is an integrated system of location, detection, ventilation, suppression, explosion control, and operations.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The module emphasizes integrated controls. Detection, ventilation, suppression, explosion control, location, and separation interact.

slides.title: Up Next
slides.next_module_title: Codes, Standards, And OCP Ready™ Application
slides.thank_you_message: Thank you for completing Module 3: Safety Hazards, Mitigation, And Operations.

slides.title: Codes, Standards, And OCP Ready™ Application
slides.subtitle: How to navigate local codes, international standards, insurer guidance, and OCP Ready™ ESS parameters.

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Standards Path You Will Apply

source_refs.source: slides.pptx

slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to...
objectives: Explain why ESS compliance is layered across standards, local authorities, and insurers.
Name the major North American standards and codes referenced in the white paper.
Describe how IEC 62933 and related international standards fit the ESS landscape.
Use the OCP Ready™ ESS matrix as a planning checklist rather than a late form.

slides.title: Why Standards Fragment

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt

slides.subtitle: A design can meet one standard and still face local or insurer constraints.

bullets.text: NFPA and ICC codes become enforceable when adopted by governmental authorities.
Jurisdictions can elevate requirements and influence final acceptance.
Codes can invoke UL, CSA, ASHRAE, ANSI, IEC, and other standards.
Insurance or AHJ requirements found late can force redesign.

figure.alt: Layered compliance model from product evidence through operations.
figure.caption: Compliance is a layered approval environment, not a single checkbox.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 720 350" role="img" aria-label="Layered compliance model" style="width:100%;height:auto;max-height:350px;">
  <rect width="720" height="350" fill="#ffffff"/>
  <text x="34" y="42" font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="22" font-weight="700" fill="#343895">Compliance Is Layered</text>
  <g font-family="Open Sans, Arial" font-size="15" font-weight="700" text-anchor="middle">
    <rect x="110" y="84" width="500" height="42" rx="8" fill="#EAF4E1" stroke="#8DC63F"/><text x="360" y="111" fill="#343895">Product certification and test evidence</text>
    <rect x="132" y="132" width="456" height="42" rx="8" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#8DC63F"/><text x="360" y="159" fill="#343895">Installation code and fire code</text>
    <rect x="154" y="180" width="412" height="42" rx="8" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#8DC63F"/><text x="360" y="207" fill="#343895">Local amendments and AHJ acceptance</text>
    <rect x="176" y="228" width="368" height="42" rx="8" fill="#F4F8F1" stroke="#343895"/><text x="360" y="255" fill="#343895">Insurance and loss-prevention requirements</text>
    <rect x="198" y="276" width="324" height="42" rx="8" fill="#FFFFFF" stroke="#343895"/><text x="360" y="303" fill="#343895">Operations and emergency response</text>
  </g>
</svg>

slides.title: North American Standards Stack

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Different references answer different parts of the ESS question.

cards.title: NFPA 855
cards.body: Installation and utilization safety for ESS, including location, ventilation, suppression, explosion control, and lifecycle steps.

cards.title: NFPA 70 / 70E
cards.body: Electrical code and workplace electrical safety requirements.

cards.title: ICC / IFC
cards.body: Model fire and building code context that may be adopted or amended locally.

cards.title: UL 9540
cards.body: Safety standard for energy storage systems and equipment.

cards.title: UL 9540A / CSA C800
cards.body: Thermal runaway and large-scale fire test evidence paths.

cards.title: Proposed NFPA 800
cards.body: Battery safety code expected to address storage, transportation, recycling, and disposal.

slides.title: IEC 62933 In The Standards Stack

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: The IEC 62933 family provides a system-level international frame for ESS.
rows: Efficiency, response time, capacity, and lifecycle benchmarking.
Electrical, chemical, mechanical, and fire safety considerations.
Thermal runaway prevention, emergency protocols, and system protection.
Operational safety for grid or microgrid interaction.
Battery safety, power automation communications, and EMC.

slides.title: FM Global And Insurance Guidance

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf
ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt

slides.subtitle: Loss-prevention expectations can shape design decisions.

bullets.text: DC battery systems.
Data centers and related facilities.
Lithium-ion battery energy storage systems.
Bring insurance review into location, separation, protection, and response planning early.

figure.alt: Summit standards framework visual including NFPA, UL, IEC, FM Global, and risk guidance.
figure.caption: Summit slide 7: standards and risk guidance overlap across regions and deployment types.

slides.title: Reading The OCP Ready™ ESS Matrix

source_refs.source: WHITE PAPER_OCP READY REQUIREMENTS FOR ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS_FINAL.pdf

slides.subtitle: Read Appendix A as a planning checklist.

bullets.text: Code requirements are the minimum acceptable level of compliance.
White and grey space decisions should consider flammable materials and isolation.
Gas and smoke detection should follow applicable fire codes and risk evidence.
Automated systems and enhanced ventilation may be needed for some technologies.
Capacity, location, spacing, and containment requirements link back to NFPA 855.

figure.alt: Appendix A OCP Ready™ Requirements for ESS Matrix.
figure.caption: Appendix A turns the white paper into practical OCP Ready™ requirement categories.

slides.title: Deployment Workflow And Community Path

source_refs.source: slides.pptx
ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt

slides.subtitle: Use the white paper as a sequence, not a late-stage checklist.

figure.alt: Silent animation of an ESS deployment workflow moving from use case and deployment classification through hazards, standards, early review, evidence, operations, and OCP community feedback.

slides.caption: After the evidence package and operations plan are in place, feed lessons back into the OCP Energy Storage Systems project so the community guidance improves.

slides.title: Application Summary

source_refs.source: slides.pptx

items: ESS compliance is layered across standards, codes, authorities, insurers, and operations.
No single standard is the whole answer for every deployment.
The OCP Ready™ ESS matrix translates requirements into concrete planning categories.
A practical workflow starts with use case and architecture, then maps hazards and evidence.
The OCP ESS project gives the community a place to continue refining deployment guidance.

slides.title: Check The Application Path
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: How should the OCP Ready™ ESS matrix be used during deployment planning?

choices.text: As a planning checklist that connects requirements to location, hazards, controls, and evidence.
As a late-stage form completed after all design decisions are frozen.
As a replacement for local codes, AHJ review, and insurer requirements.
As a vendor ranking system for selecting one ESS technology.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The matrix is most useful as an early planning checklist, not a late paperwork exercise.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Use the matrix early. It helps connect deployment choices, hazards, evidence, standards, and approval work.

questions.prompt: Which sources can shape the final compliance path for a data center ESS deployment?

choices.text: Codes and standards adopted by governmental authorities.
UL, CSA, NFPA, ICC, IEC, and related technical standards.
AHJ conditions, insurer guidance, and local amendments.
One universal standard that overrides every site-specific requirement.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. ESS compliance is layered across standards, codes, authorities, insurers, and site operations.
questions.feedback_incorrect: No single standard is the whole answer. Local adoption, AHJ conditions, insurer guidance, and technical standards all matter.

slides.title: Course Complete

source_refs.source: ESS in Hyperscale & AI Data Centers OCP Ready  Requirements Update.txt
